title: Insulating cold water pipes with foam lagging
subtitle: Fit foam pipe lagging to exposed cold water pipes to prevent freezing and reduce condensation
excerpt: How to fit split foam pipe lagging to 15 mm and 22 mm copper or plastic cold water pipes in a loft, garage, or external wall, covering cutting to length, fitting around bends and fittings, and sealing the joins.
sourceNotes: Collins Complete DIY Manual. Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics. BS 6700 (domestic water services).

glossaryTerms.definition: A pre-split polyethylene or rubber foam tube that slides over a pipe to reduce heat transfer. Available in bore sizes to match 15 mm and 22 mm pipe, and in wall thicknesses of 13 mm (standard) and 25 mm (frost-risk). The split runs the full length so the lagging can be opened and clipped over the pipe without disconnecting it.

Moisture from the air that forms on the outside of a cold pipe when warm, humid air touches the cold pipe surface. Lagging prevents this by keeping the pipe surface above the dew point of the room air. It is the reverse of pipe insulation for hot pipes, which retains heat; cold pipe lagging excludes warmth.

content.text: Cold water pipes in unheated spaces (lofts, garages, under floors, within external walls) are at risk of freezing in cold weather.
slows the rate of heat loss from the air surrounding the pipe to the water inside it, delaying the onset of freezing. It also prevents
dripping from cold pipes onto joists or insulation in a warm, damp room.

Foam pipe lagging in the correct bore size (15 mm bore for 15 mm pipe, 22 mm bore for 22 mm pipe). Use 25 mm wall thickness in a loft or garage with significant frost risk; 13 mm is sufficient for a pipe in a heated internal wall or floor void. Self-adhesive foam lagging needs no tape; plain foam lagging needs masking tape or duct tape at every join and at intervals along the run to prevent opening.
Step 1, measure and cut the lagging sections

Measure each straight run of pipe between fittings. Cut the lagging to length with the utility knife, cutting at 90 degrees to the bore. Straight cuts butt tightly against each other at inline joints. At bends, cut each section at 45 degrees where it meets the curve so the two angled ends close together around the bend without a gap. Mark the cut angle with the pencil before cutting.

Open the split along the length of the lagging and press it over the pipe. The foam springs closed around the pipe. On self-adhesive lagging, press the split edges together firmly along the full length to activate the adhesive strip. On plain foam lagging, apply a strip of masking tape along the split every 300 mm to hold it closed. Butt adjacent sections tightly together and tape across each joint.
Step 3, fit around fittings and bends

At a 90-degree bend, cut the lagging at 45 degrees on each approaching run so the two mitre cuts close together around the outside of the bend. At a compression or push-fit fitting (elbow, tee, or coupling), cut the lagging sections to butt up tightly to each side of the fitting body. Wrap the exposed fitting with offcuts of lagging or pipe-lagging tape. A gap at a fitting is a weak point for frost.
Step 4, seal the lagging in frost-risk areas

In a loft or garage where the temperature can drop below zero, apply tape across every butt joint and at both ends of each section. Any gap, however small, allows cold air to reach the pipe surface directly. Where a pipe passes through an insulated loft floor, wrap the lagging all the way down through the void: a bare pipe in the warm ceiling space below the insulation is warmer, but the short unlagged section in the void is the weak point.

For the pipe section that enters the house through an external wall, use foam lagging rated for outdoor use, or wrap the pipe in mineral wool and cover it with split conduit. Where the pipe runs inside a wall cavity and foam lagging will not fit, use trace heating tape. Trace tape is a low-wattage electric element clipped alongside the pipe that warms it in cold weather.

Inspect lagging in the loft and garage each autumn before the first cold spell. Foam lagging becomes brittle and cracks after five to ten years, especially in a hot loft. Replace any section that has split open or pulled apart at the joints. Mice will chew through foam lagging in a loft: replace chewed sections and fit a mouse excluder at the loft hatch.

attrs.symptom: Lagging keeps popping open along the split after fitting
attrs.cause: The foam has relaxed and the split edges are not held together.
attrs.fix: Apply masking tape or duct tape along the full length of the split, not just at intervals. Self-adhesive lagging should be pressed firmly along the split immediately after fitting; if the adhesive did not bond, add external tape.

attrs.symptom: Pipe still freezes after lagging is fitted
attrs.cause: The lagging has a gap at a fitting or where it passes through the loft floor, or the wall thickness is insufficient for the frost exposure.
attrs.fix: Check every joint and fitting for gaps. Replace 13 mm lagging with 25 mm lagging in the most exposed section. If the pipe runs along an external wall face in an unheated space, the lagging alone may not be enough: fit trace heating tape as well.
